schools.summary: A daybreak session ratifying the night trial (Luke 22:66); the Jews had lost the power of death and needed a civil charge; Lightfoot on the violated canons.
Jerome on the priests' evil zeal watching all night; Chrysostom: they killed him openly to destroy his reputation; Origen on binding him who looses the bound.
Henry: the power of capital punishment taken from the Jews, so they deliver him to Pilate.
Calvin: they keep the form of justice, hurrying to Pilate at sunrise though their feet ran to evil.
The re-binding (bound in the garden, John 18:12); Ellicott's sketch of Pilate the procurator under Tiberius; Clarke on his deposition and suicide.
Gill: bound for security and for greater reproach, to be taken at once for a malefactor.
Cyril of Jerusalem applies the prophecy of one 'led as a present to the king' to Jesus sent to Herod (Luke 23:7).
'Repented' = metameletheis, regret not saving metanoia (2 Corinthians 7:9-10); the question whether Judas expected a miracle; his confession clears Christ's character.
Calvin: not reformation but uneasiness; God opens the reprobate's eyes to feel misery without true repentance.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the thirty pieces foretold in Zechariah 11:13.

debates.question: Had Judas expected a different outcome?

sides.position: He hoped Jesus would deliver himself by a miracle
Simple remorse at the consequences is enough to explain it

schools.summary: Judas's confession a remarkable proof of Christ's innocence; the variant 'righteous'; 'see thou to it' is the sentiment of Cain (Robertson); Ellicott on the priests' deeper guilt and Judas's despair.
Calvin: the priests, warned by Judas's example and his witness to innocence, remain seared in conscience.
Wesley: they could digest innocent blood yet scrupled at the treasury.
Cyril of Jerusalem: out of their own mouths — if the price is polluted, so is the deed.
He flung the money into the naos; 'hanged himself' (2 Samuel 17:23) harmonised with Acts 1:18 by want of skill (Ellicott); Lightfoot's devil-strangling reading; Clarke's middle 'strangled'.
Calvin: the price by which Satan flatters then drives to fury; self-murderers diminish not God's wrath.
Cyril of Jerusalem compares Gospel and prophecy on the casting down of the silver.

debates.question: How is 'hanged himself' reconciled with Acts 1:18?

sides.position: He hanged himself; the fall and bursting followed (want of skill or broken rope)
The devil strangled him and threw him down headlong

schools.summary: The Corban could not receive blood-money (Deuteronomy 23:18); the hair-splitting rabbis (Mark 7:11) balk here while adjudging innocent blood — hypocrisy (Clarke).
Origen/Augustine allegorise the potter's field as the world bought by Christ's blood, the strangers as the Gentiles at repose (Romans 6:4); Jerome on the Jews' perversity becoming Gentile salvation.
Calvin: if the treasury was sacred why take money out of it? the pollution came from themselves.
Cyril of Jerusalem: if the price is polluted, so is the deed.
Reconcile with Acts 1:18 (Judas furnished the means); the field near Hinnom (Jeremiah 19:2), a worked-out quarry for burying strangers; Ellicott's 'subtle fitness'.
Clarke and Wesley: the money Christ was sold for becomes a means of mercy, the field giving rest to strangers as his blood gives rest to souls.
Aceldama (Acts 1:19) outside the walls; named for blood-money and for Judas's blood; 'unto this day' implies an interval before writing (cf. Matthew 28:15).
Calvin: God brings hidden crimes to light — the name given as if by his command, a perpetual memorial.
Clarke: an eternal monument of Judas's treachery, the priests' perfidy, and Christ's innocence.
The quotation is from Zechariah 11:13; theories for 'Jeremy' — Jeremiah first in the roll, copyist error, quotation from memory (cf. Mark 1:2 referring a composite to Isaiah).
Calvin and Wesley: 'Jeremy' is a mistake for Zechariah, but the substance — the despised shepherd valued at thirty pieces — is rightly applied to Christ.
Cyril of Jerusalem dwells on the exactness of the prophecy of the thirty pieces.

debates.question: Why is the quotation attributed to Jeremiah when it is from Zechariah?

sides.position: A copyist's or memory slip; it should be Zechariah
Jeremiah stood first in the prophetic roll and named the whole
The text is original and correct as 'Jeremy'

schools.summary: Barnes on the Zechariah original — the despised shepherd valued at a slave's price, cast to the potter; Gill harmonises 'I gave'/'they gave' and 'house of the Lord'.
Calvin: the clause confirms God's providence — the priests unconsciously fulfil an ancient prediction.
Cyril of Jerusalem resolves 'house of the Lord' with 'potter's field'.
The priests substituted a political charge (Luke 23:2); the kingship question alone mattered; John's dialogue on the kingdom 'not of this world' (John 18:36).
Origen on the Judge of all humbling himself; Hilary on the two answers; Jerome/Chrysostom: he answered Pilate but was silent to the unworthy priests.
Calvin: he stood condemned at a profane bar that we might approach the heavenly throne — salvation in the doctrine of the cross (1 Corinthians 1:23).
The calm silence of conscious innocence against the clamour of accusers (Luke 23:2; cf. Matthew 26:63).
Calvin: he had a defence at hand but voluntarily abstained; the judge saw his integrity without it.
Clarke: the accusations confuted themselves and needed no answer.
Barnes: the priests were accusers not witnesses; Pilate refers to the pressing charges of stirring sedition from Galilee to Jerusalem (Luke 23:5).
Gill: Pilate asked, seeing Jesus made no reply, to draw something out of him.
The complete silence amazed Pilate (the precise Greek 'not even one word'); what affected him was that Jesus returned no railing; the charge was absurd on its face.
Clarke: silence under calumny shows the utmost magnanimity and was predicted (Isaiah 53:7); Pilate, with no interest to serve, was deeply affected.
The only recorded instance of the custom, of uncertain origin (concession to the Jews or Roman feast-practice); at the passover.
Chrysostom: Pilate contrives to rescue Christ; Hilary: Barabbas ('son of a father') shadows preferring Antichrist; Leo: Pilate not guiltless, resigning his jurisdiction.
Calvin: the priests' insatiable cruelty and the people's furious obstinacy — not content to kill the innocent unless they also freed a murderer.
Origen: the people like wild beasts prefer Barabbas — where Jesus is not, there are strifes.
Barabbas a notorious insurrectionist and murderer (Luke 23:19; Mark 15:7); the textual evidence for 'Jesus Barabbas', the Holy Name later blotted out; Lightfoot on the common rabbinic name.

debates.question: Was the prisoner's full name 'Jesus Barabbas'?

sides.position: Likely yes — strong though not decisive manuscript evidence, the Name later erased

schools.summary: Pilate, sure of Christ's innocence (Luke 23:14), set him against a notorious criminal expecting the people to prefer him; 'catching at straws' to escape responsibility (Robertson, Ellicott).
Gill: the choice was put to the multitude; Pilate added 'called Christ', perhaps to make them likelier to ask for the Messiah.
Pilate saw the real motive was envy at Christ's popularity; knowing it, he was bound to release him, yet did not (Barnes, Robertson).
Clarke: an upright judge should have dispersed the mob; beware the spark of malice (Proverbs 27:4); Wesley: they envied him because the people magnified him.
The judgment-seat on the pavement (John 19:13); Pilate's wife (Procula, a likely proselyte); her dream increased his superstitious fears (Robertson); Clarke: God testified Christ's innocence to her.
Calvin: the dream was God's inspiration not the devil's — God's many attestations of Christ's innocence, that he might be seen to suffer in our room.

debates.question: Was the dream from God or from Satan?

sides.position: From God, attesting Christ's innocence
From Satan to hinder the redemption (rejected)

schools.summary: The rulers urged the crowd to demand Barabbas; those who cried 'hosanna' now cry 'crucify' (Acts 3:14); the Sanhedrin whispering through the crowd.
Calvin: the people not of themselves hostile but sold to gratify the priests — how pernicious the influence of wicked men over the changeful multitude.
Clarke contrasts the murderer Barabbas with Jesus who went about doing good.
The renewed question after the priests had worked the crowd; Barnes on the choice between Jesus and Barabbas.
Clarke: the fickle crowd, lately hailing the Son of David, now prefer a murderer at the priests' instigation — dangerous wicked priests.
Origen: the synagogue's bill of divorcement, the Gentiles called in her place (cf. Romans 11:11).
Pilate pressing the question hoping for Jesus, but the Sunday 'Hosanna' becomes Friday's 'crucify'; the first direct demand for crucifixion; Pilate shirking responsibility.
Calvin: Pilate appeals to their honour — Christ's death would disgrace them — but their cruelty does not abate.
Wesley: they chose Barabbas's punishment and furnished Rome a precedent that soon turned on themselves.
The question proves no crime was shown; Pilate asked three times and proposed to scourge and release (Luke 23:22) — weakness that only stirred the mob; a flickering conscience (Robertson).
Clarke: Pilate saw nothing deserving condemnation, yet the people, unable to answer, were set on his death.
The Mosaic hand-washing over unsolved murder (Deuteronomy 21:6; Psalm 26:6); but it did not free Pilate, bound as magistrate to acquit the innocent and able to disperse the mob.
Calvin: like a sailor giving way to the storm, Pilate lays aside his office; a few drops cannot wash away the crime.
Wesley: the custom was common among heathens and Jews.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): glory in the cross, the power of God to the saved (1 Corinthians 1:23-24).

The imprecation answered in the fall of Jerusalem (a million perished, crosses lacking); but only a fraction were present and some rulers dissented (Luke 23:51); no excuse for race hatred (Robertson).
Calvin: inconsiderate zeal cutting themselves off from pardon — a warning against rash judgment.
Wesley: literally fulfilled on many who cried it and on their children.
The Roman flagellum, a brutal preliminary to crucifixion (distinct from the Jewish stripes, 2 Corinthians 11:24); Pilate lost interest seeing no friends in the crowd.
Henry: Barabbas delivered that we might be delivered — here the upright a ransom for the transgressor (reversing Proverbs 21:18).
Calvin: Pilate condemns against his will the man he calls innocent, that the Son of God be condemned as our surety; Gill: Barabbas an emblem of those for whom Christ suffered.
Cyril of Jerusalem cites 'I gave my back to the scourges' (Isaiah 50:6).
The praetorium and the cohort (a tenth of a legion); heathen soldiers despising Jew and condemned alike; Augustine harmonises scarlet/purple (Mark 15:17).
Jerome/Remigius: the scarlet robe as the Lord's blood-stained flesh, the thorns as the Gentiles' sins (Romans 8:3); Origen on the reed as the world's broken kingdom.
Chrysostom: not one member but his whole body suffered; the devil entering in triumph.
Calvin: we deserved abhorrence, but Christ bore every reproach to present us pure and restore God's image.
The chlamys/paludamentum, a cast-off military cloak (possibly Herod's, Luke 23:11); scarlet/purple reconciled (Mark 15:17); the kermes dye.
Clarke/Wesley: a robe such as kings wore, probably old and tattered.
Gill: stripping him was part of the low estate Christ submitted to.
The thorn-wreath (Zizyphus Spina Christi) made like a victor's garland, the reed a mock sceptre, the salute copying 'Hail Caesar'; the crown meant to ridicule though it pained.
Gill on platting the common white thorns and the feigned worship of bowing the knee.
Spitting as deepest contempt (Matthew 26:67); smiting with the reed pressed the thorns deeper; his visage more marred than any man's (Isaiah 52:14).
Clarke: Christ preaches to the kings of the earth that their sceptres are but reeds with which they shall be smitten at his tribunal.
His own garments restored; John's 'Behold the man' and the final sentence (John 19:5); the seamless tunic over lacerated flesh; Lightfoot's Sanhedrin and Talmudic notices.
Jerome: scourged in our garments, crucified in his own; Augustine harmonises the gall and myrrh (Mark 15:23); Chrysostom: he suffered outside the city, the common sacrifice (Hebrews 13:12).
Henry: led as a lamb to the slaughter; daily take up the cross and follow.
Jesus bore the cross first, then Simon was compelled (John 19:17; Matthew 5:41) when weakness overcame him; Simon father of Alexander and Rufus, perhaps a secret disciple (Mark 15:21).
Calvin: Christ bending under the cross proves the lamb was sacrificed; a mean peasant seized for the detestable burden.
Wesley: he bore it himself till he sank under it.
Golgotha = 'skull' (Calvary), the place of execution, named for skulls or its shape; outside the wall yet near the city (John 19:20; Hebrews 13:12); Robertson favours Gordon's Calvary.
Calvin from Hebrews 13:12: as the sacrifices were burnt outside the camp (Leviticus 16:27), Christ went outside the gate, bearing our curse to atone.
Henry: where criminals were sacrificed to government's justice, Christ was sacrificed to God's justice.
Leo magnifies the mystery of God's mercy in the Passion.

The drink was the customary stupefying potion (Proverbs 31:6); 'gall' = wormwood (Proverbs 5:4); the accounts with Mark's 'myrrh' are reconcilable; the refusal to drink to the dregs the Father's cup (John 18:11).
Calvin: the potion hastened death by evacuating blood; Christ refused not from dislike of bitterness but to show he advanced calmly to death by the Father's command.
Wesley: the myrrh-wine deadened criminals' pain, but Christ refused it, determining to bear the full force of his sufferings.
The clinical horror of crucifixion (Barnes, Clarke), the most ignominious Roman death; the garments parted fulfilling Psalm 22:18 (Clarke deems the Matthew citation a later gloss from John).
Augustine's wood-typology (ark, Moses' rod) leading to the cross; the Gloss on the four quarters = the Church; Raban allegorises the cross's parts.
Calvin: the Son stripped that we, clothed in his righteousness, may stand boldly with the angels.
Chrysostom: crucified on a lofty cross to purify the air; the garments shared only of the poorest condemned.

outliers.note: Traces the saving 'wood' through Scripture (ark, Moses' rod, the tree that sweetened the water) up to the wood of the cross.

schools.summary: The four soldiers watched to prevent rescue or removal of the body, per Roman custom; Robertson's 'comedy at the foot of the Cross, the tragedy of the ages.'
The titulus naming the charge, in three languages (John); the Gospels' variations reconciled; Ellicott sees Pilate's 'rough tenderness' in recognising Christ's kingship.
Jerome and Remigius: divinely provided that even in death they could not avoid having him as King; Origen: the true High Priest bears 'King of the Jews' on his cross.
Calvin: the three languages providential — Pilate an unwitting herald of Christ (like Caiaphas the prophet, John 11:51), proclaiming the gospel to all nations.
'Robbers,' not thieves (the word of Barabbas), probably of his band; numbered with transgressors (Isaiah 53:12), placed in the midst as the worst.
Hilary, Leo, Remigius: the two robbers figure the division of all mankind at the judgment, believers to the right, unbelievers to the left.
Calvin: the lowest disgrace, ranked chief among robbers (Isaiah 53:12), revealing both the weight of God's wrath against sin and Christ's love in being classed with the wicked.
Chrysostom: he stays on the cross because he is the Son of God, come to be crucified; Pseudo-Chrysostom hears the devil's wilderness taunt ('If thou be the Son of God') repeated.
Jerome: a demonic suggestion; Leo: 'The Lord hath reigned from the tree'; Hilary: the cross an offence even to the faithful.
A lounging crowd wagging their heads (Psalm 22:7), rulers and people shameless in mocking a dying man.
Calvin: the reviling as God-forsaken (Isaiah 53:4) was crueller than all tortures, a true part of the cup, that our faith not be shaken when men taunt us so.

outliers.note: Hears in 'If thou be the Son of God, come down' the echo of Satan's wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:6).

schools.summary: The perverted temple-saying turned to derision; 'If thou be the Son of God' echoes the devil's wilderness taunt (Matthew 4:3); Ellicott: to accept the challenge would be to disown his Sonship.
Calvin: they triumph prematurely, not waiting the three days; they demand he prove his Sonship by an act that would deny it — to remain on the cross was the work of salvation.
Gill: the chief priests, forgetting office and humanity, lead the mockery instead of restraining the mob, exulting over the Christ they condemned.
Robertson: the Sanhedrin itself, mocking (empaizontes) like silly children; Ellicott: nearly all who condemned him came to feast on his sufferings; Clarke doubts the Pharisees joined.
Ellicott and Robertson: the mockers bear unconscious witness — he saved others by not saving himself (Matthew 10:39); their promise to believe plainly false.
Clarke: the contradictions that offend Jews and libertines are what a Christian adores — the Savior who dies to be the victim of salvation.
Calvin: they demand a Christ opposite to the prophets' suffering Messiah (Isaiah 53:2); the highest reason for faith is that he chose the cross for us.
Ellicott: the priests unwittingly quote Psalm 22:8, not reading it as Messianic; Clarke on the Septuagint wording; Robertson notes they add 'now.'
Calvin: Satan's sharp arrow — that delayed help proves God's abandonment; but Christ, though beloved Son, was not delivered until he paid the price of our salvation.
Augustine: the plural for the singular (as Hebrews 11:33 of Daniel); Jerome/Chrysostom: both reviled at first, one converted when the signs increased.
Barnes harmonises with Luke (both reviled, then one relented; or one attributed to both); corrects 'cast in his teeth' to 'reproached.'
Calvin: by synecdoche, the reviling of even a dying robber completes Christ's reproach on every hand; Origen: the saved thief a sign of late-believing sinners.

debates.question: Did both robbers revile Christ, against Luke's one penitent?

sides.position: Both reviled at first, one later converted by the signs
A plural-for-singular idiom; only one reviled

schools.summary: No eclipse was possible at the Passover full moon — a supernatural darkness; the pagan testimonies (Phlegon, Dionysius) cited but weighed critically; probably over Judea (Barnes, Clarke).
Origen allegorises: as Egypt was dark while Israel had light, Judea is darkened for its sin while the Church everywhere has light (Romans 11:25).
Chrysostom: the very 'sign from heaven' they demanded (Matthew 12:39); Pseudo-Chrysostom: creation withdrew its light from the crime against the Creator.
Calvin: a localized darkness was the greater miracle and judgment, rousing them to the design of Christ's death; redemption weighty as if the sun had fallen.
Henry: during the three hours Jesus wrestled with the powers of darkness, bearing the Father's displeasure against sin.

debates.question: Was the darkness over the whole world or only over Judea?

sides.position: Confined to Judea (the greater miracle and judgment)
Over the whole world (the pagan testimonies)

outliers.note: Reads the darkness over Judea against the light over the Church as Egypt's plague-darkness against Israel's light (Romans 11:25).

schools.summary: Calvin: the chief conflict — he bore in his soul the punishment due to us, placed as guilty at God's judgment-seat, yet 'My God' shows faith held firm even in the sense of estrangement.
Quoted from Psalm 22:1 in Aramaic; the bearing of the curse (Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:21), the depth of soul-suffering as he was made sin though sinless (Robertson).
Hilary and the Gloss guard the union: the desertion is the dying man's weakness; God withdrew protection but did not break the union; Christ laments as representing us.
Clarke/Wesley: deity did not depart (lest the atonement lose its merit), only restraining consolation so the human nature felt all its sufferings.

sides.position: He bore in his soul the judgment and wrath due to our sin, yet faith held firm
God withdrew protective comfort but the union of natures was unbroken

schools.summary: Clarke, Ellicott: honest misunderstanding by Hellenistic Jews, the Elijah-expectation (Malachi 4:5) fastening on the similar sound; Barnes reads it as deliberate derision.
Calvin: a deliberate mockery turning his prayer into slander — Satan's surest method, to dissuade the godly from calling on God.

debates.question: Did the bystanders mishear 'Eli' as Elijah, or mock deliberately?

sides.position: Honest misunderstanding
Deliberate derision

schools.summary: Prompted by 'I thirst' (John 19:28); the soldiers' sour wine on a hyssop-reed; unlike the refused narcotic, Jesus drank this, fulfilling Psalm 69:21; offered in rough pity.
Wesley: given not in derision but with friendly design.
Calvin: the vinegar perhaps offered to annoy, yet held to his thirsting mouth.
Barnes reads derision; Ellicott hears expectation of Elijah (Malachi 4:5); Robertson a pious-sounding excuse; both reject the spear-thrust interpolation from John 19:34.

sides.position: Continued derision
Eager expectation of Elijah

outliers.note: Rejects the manuscript addition of the spear-thrust (John 19:34) here as a scribal copy that wrecks v.50's context.

themes: let us see whether Elias come

schools.summary: Henry: the loud cry shows life not forced but freely surrendered, the Priest offering himself the Sacrifice (Isaiah 63:1); a true death, the soul separated, the price of atonement (Hebrews 9:15).
Augustine: Christ took our death that we might be freed by his, exalting mortals by despising death.
The loud cry proved he died by a free laying-down of life, not mere exhaustion (John 10:18); the two sayings Luke and John record.

Henry's four senses: the dissolving of Christ's body-temple, the unveiling of OT mysteries (2 Cor 3:13), the uniting of Jew and Gentile (Eph 2:14), and the new and living way to God (Hebrews 10:19-20).
Origen: the outer veil rent to publish the hidden mysteries; the inner veil to be removed when the perfect comes (1 Cor 13:10).
Hilary and Leo: the earth quaked unable to contain such a body, the rocks rent by the piercing Word; let the rocks of unbelief be rent.
The veil between holy and most holy rent by invisible power at the evening sacrifice, the priests as witnesses.
The rent rocks opened the graves; 'slept' = died (Daniel 12:2); Lightfoot: a peculiar miracle beyond the quake; Ellicott weighs the difficulty and links it to 1 Peter 3:19.
Origen allegorises the graves as sinful souls raised to be bodies of saints.
Hilary: the bands of death loosed, Christ illumining death's darkness; Cyril: many raised without touching him, unlike Elisha (2 Kings 13:21).

outliers.note: Connects the risen saints with Peter's testimony that Christ preached to the spirits in prison (1 Peter 3:19; 4:6).

schools.summary: 'After his resurrection' fixes the order — Christ the firstfruits rose first (1 Cor 15:20); Barnes: their purpose to attest the Messiah, their later fate unrevealed.
Calvin: 'the holy city' so called for God's adoption, not its corrupt inhabitants; Cyril: the same city, the wonder a proof of resurrection.
Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott: from the heathen's standpoint, 'a son of God' = a god-like hero or righteous man (Luke's softer report; Wisdom 2:13-18); Robertson: probably more, perhaps saving faith.
Lightfoot reads it higher ('this was the Messias'); Calvin/Wesley/Clarke: God vindicates the Son by a heathen's confession amid the rulers' mockery.

debates.question: How much did the centurion mean by 'the Son of God'?

sides.position: A heathen's tribute — a god-like hero or righteous man
A true confession of the Messiah / Son of God

schools.summary: Gill: the faithful women a rebuke to Peter and the disciples who fled, only John present.
Barnes, Clarke, Robertson: the women's constancy outshines the disciples' flight; faithful in Galilee (Luke 8:2-3), last at the cross, beholding from afar.
The three named women identified — Mary Magdalene (Mark 16:9), Mary mother of James and Joses, and Salome mother of Zebedee's sons (Mark 15:40); Clarke's 'four Marys.'
Gill and Lightfoot canvass the rabbinic confusions and whether Magdalene was Mary of Bethany; the named witnesses anchor the testimony.

Remigius, Jerome, Chrysostom: Joseph's righteous merit fitting him for the office; rich, that he might dare approach Pilate; bold to ask and bury at risk of his life; the clean linen a pure heart embracing Jesus.
A counsellor now openly avowing Christ; the honourable burial fulfilling Isaiah 53:9, 'with the rich in his death.'
Henry reads the rich man's burial as the fulfilment of prophecy.

outliers.note: Notes that the Saviour was laid in another man's tomb because he died for the salvation of others.

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph went boldly, not ashamed of Christ crucified though sure to incur the Jews' persecution.
Lightfoot on the law (Deuteronomy 21:23) and the council's grave for the condemned; only the magistrate could release the body; Ellicott: Pilate's lingering reverence in granting it.
John adds the spices (John 19:40); a hundred pounds of myrrh and aloes; a second embalming intended (Luke 23:56); Ellicott on the sindōn (muslin) cloth.
Calvin: the Synoptists glance briefly, but enough to show an honourable burial, brought about by God's secret providence.
The new tomb (no other body) prevents confusion at the resurrection (contrast 2 Kings 13:21); the rich man's grave fulfils Isaiah 53:9; the rock and great stone make theft impossible.
Gill: the new tomb ordered to confirm the resurrection, lest another's body be thought raised.
Calvin: the women sat by the tomb resolving to honour the burial later (Luke 23:56), their minds filled with the better odour of Christ's death.
Ellicott and Clarke: the women watched the entombment and marked the place, preparing spices before the Sabbath, that they might come at dawn (Luke 23:56).
Jerome: their malice pursues Christ beyond death; Hilary: sealing the tomb is folly and unbelief; Raban on the Parasceve and the sixth-day parallel; Augustine on the three days and the Trinity.
Chrysostom: against their will they demonstrate the truth — the watched, sealed tomb makes the resurrection irrefragable.
Henry and the expositors echo the irony: the priests' precautions secure the proof of the resurrection.

outliers.note: Turns the priests' plot into proof: the watched, sealed tomb makes fraud impossible and the resurrection certain.

schools.summary: Robertson: the rulers knew the prediction (Matthew 12:40), rejecting Meyer's 'legend'; why did the disciples forget and the rulers remember? — grief versus suspicion; Ellicott: the priests understood the temple-saying.
Clarke: the wicked, fulfilling their own counsels, subserve Christianity — their precautions give the resurrection the fullest evidence.
The request concedes the burial and tomb; Barnes: 'until the third day' shows 'three days' = parts of three; Robertson: the two 'errors' are belief in his Messiahship and his resurrection.
Cyril: the sealed tomb prefigured as 'a fountain sealed' (Song of Songs 4:12), the Saviour the fountain of living water.
Robertson/Ellicott: an imperative, 'Take ye a guard' of Roman soldiers (custodia); the governor's contempt for the Jews; Clarke/Gill: leave to use the temple cohort (Acts 4:1).
Calvin: the permission bound them the more — with Pilate's signet they shut their own mouths as they shut the tomb.

debates.question: Was the guard Roman soldiers or the temple police?

sides.position: Roman soldiers granted by Pilate (custodia)
The temple cohort, by Pilate's leave

schools.summary: The stone sealed (as Daniel 6:17) with cords and the seal of Pilate or the priests; the guard against theft, the seal against the guard's corruption; Barnes: enemies took every precaution against fraud, so authenticating the resurrection.
Clarke: human policy did everything to prevent a resurrection it thereby most authenticated.
Cyril: the 'fountain sealed' (Song of Songs 4:12), the sealed tomb a figure of the Saviour.
